---
title: Wooden cutting boards
date: 2024-03-09
tags: [cooking, woodworking, kitchen]
---

End-grain boards are kinder to knives than edge-grain, because the blade parts the wood fibers instead of cutting across them. Hard maple is the traditional choice, but cherry and walnut both make beautiful boards if you can keep them dry.

I build mine from offcuts glued into a long blank, then crosscut and re-glued so the end grain faces up. Flattening such a board by hand is tedious; I take it to a drum sander in a friend's shop when I can. A shallow juice groove on one face and generous chamfers on the edges finish the piece.

The maintenance is simple but non-negotiable. I wash the board with soap and water, never let it soak, and oil it monthly with food-safe mineral oil followed by a paste of beeswax and more oil. A well-tended board outlives most kitchen gadgets.
